coredata.dc:title: An eco-friendly near infrared fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on zeolite imidazolate framework-8 for rapid determination of trace trypsin
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shan
Fu, Jinli
Zhou, Shu
Wu, Xiaodan
Tang, Sisi
Zhao, Pengfei
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: This report depicts an environment-friendly molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for rapid determination of trypsin based on new near infrared (NIR) fluorescence carbon dots and metal–organic frameworks. The novel NIR fluorescence carbon dots were facilely synthesized from green plants by one-step solvothermal treatment, and the imprinted sensor was prepared by sol–gel under mild condition. The imprinted NIR fluorescence sensor was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectrometry, energy disperse spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction and transmission electron microscopy. The results showed the introduction of zeolite imidazolate framework-8 can improve the fluorescence response sensitivity and response speed. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence quenching of the imprinted NIR fluorescence sensor was linear relationship with the increasing trypsin concentration in the range of 0.05–9.0 ng/L with a detection limit of 8.8 pg/L. The developed imprinted fluorescence sensor can selectively recognize trypsin in 1.5 min, which was applied to rapidly and sensitively detect trypsin in serum and urine samples successfully with the recoveries of 95.3–104.1% and 97.7–105.2%, respectively. The as-prepared imprinted fluorescence sensor provides a viable and eco-friendly method for rapidly sensitive detection of trace bio-macromolecule in complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular imprinting
Fluorescence sensor
Trypsin
Zeolite imidazolate framework-8
Near infrared fluorescence carbon dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sample preparation of human serum and urine
SEM, TEM, XRD, EDS and BET characterizations
Optosensing of CDs@ZIF-8 MIP toward trypsin
Effect of pH and incubation time
Selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8 MIP for trypsin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8@MIP.

FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8 (a); fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths (b); fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of CDs (c); UV/vis spectra of trypsin, CDs, CDs@trypsin (d) (inset, ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@MIP@trypsin); UV/vis spectrum of trypsin and fluorescence spectrum of CDs (e); schematic illustrations for the quenching mechanism (f).

SEM images of ZIF-8 (a), CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (b), CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (c). TEM images of CDs (d), ZIF-8 (e), CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (f). XRD patterns of as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (g) (inset, ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8), EDS spectra of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (h) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (i).

Fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP at different pH solutions (a), fluorescence quenching effect of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP at different pH solutions (b), fluorescence response time of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (c), the changes of fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@MIP at different response time(d).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (a) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (b) with addition of different concentrations of trypsin. Stern-Volmer plots from CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (A) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (B) with trypsin (c).

Effect of potentially interfering species and trypsin on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (a). The fluorescence quenching effect of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin and different background interfering ions (the concentration of ions solution is 10 μM) (b). The fluorescence quenching effect of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward (1) trypsin, (2) bovine serum albumin, (3) bovine hemoglobin, (4) lysozyme, (5) ovalbumin, (6) human serum albumin (c).
Results of spiked recoveries and RSDs for the determination of trypsin with the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor.
Recognition performance comparison of different methods for determination of trypsin.

head.ce:title: An eco-friendly near infrared fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on zeolite imidazolate framework-8 for rapid determination of trace trypsin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This report depicts an environment-friendly molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for rapid determination of trypsin based on new near infrared (NIR) fluorescence carbon dots and metal–organic frameworks. The novel NIR fluorescence carbon dots were facilely synthesized from green plants by one-step solvothermal treatment, and the imprinted sensor was prepared by sol–gel under mild condition. The imprinted NIR fluorescence sensor was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectrometry, energy disperse spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction and transmission electron microscopy. The results showed the introduction of zeolite imidazolate framework-8 can improve the fluorescence response sensitivity and response speed. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence quenching of the imprinted NIR fluorescence sensor was linear relationship with the increasing trypsin concentration in the range of 0.05–9.0 ng/L with a detection limit of 8.8 pg/L. The developed imprinted fluorescence sensor can selectively recognize trypsin in 1.5 min, which was applied to rapidly and sensitively detect trypsin in serum and urine samples successfully with the recoveries of 95.3–104.1% and 97.7–105.2%, respectively. The as-prepared imprinted fluorescence sensor provides a viable and eco-friendly method for rapidly sensitive detection of trace bio-macromolecule in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Trypsin, which plays an important role in cracking peptides at the C-terminal side of arginine or lysine residues, is a serine proteinase produced by pancreas [1]. Under abnormal circumstances, the secreted trypsin can cause some pancreatic diseases [2]. The level of trypsin was founded to 84.4 mg/L in urine of pancreas transplant patients, which was not detected in health human’s urine [3]. Since the level of trypsin becomes a critical biomarker of some disease caused by pancreatic dysfunction [4], monitoring the quantity of trypsin is important to prevent human from these diseases. Recent years, a growing number of ways involving electrochemical analysis [5,6], chemiluminescence spectrometry [7], photoelectrochemical analysis [8], colorimetric spectrometry [9–11], and fluorescence spectrometry [12,13] have been developed to detect trypsin. However, these methods either produced potential environment pollution or behaved low sensitivity. Therefore, it is high time to establish a green, facile, and sensitive analytical method for rapid and selective determination of trace trypsin.

In recent years, some papers on fluorescence analysis of trypsin have also been reported. Gao et al. developed an optical nano-probe using Mn-doped ZnSe quantum dots as fluorescence elements for detecting trypsin in 30 min [14]. Li et al. reported a sensitive fluorescence sensor based on glutathione-stabilized gold nano-clusters for rapid detection of trypsin with 60 min [15]. However, these commonly methods for fluorescence determination of trypsin suffer from potential biological toxicity and environmental pollution, time-consuming, and low-sensitivity. Hence, it is important to establish a green, sensitive and selective method for the rapid determination of trypsin. At present, fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer (FMIP) combining the high sensitivity of fluorescence and the high selectivity of molecularly imprinted polymer has been considered as an important approach to detect objects [16]. In addition, near infrared (NIR) fluorescence in the region of 650 ~ 900 nm has low auto-fluorescence and deeper tissue penetration ability [17,18], which provides tremendous potential for biomedical imaging in vivo owing to their immunity from interference of biological media such as tissue auto-fluorescence and scattering light [19,20]. Interestingly, carbon dot, as one of the most popular NIR fluorescence sources, possessed many outstanding features involving high water solubility, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and favorable optical properties [21,22]. As a consequence, this work designed a novel FMIP in the NIR region by using new carbon dot as fluorescence source to detect trypsin.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) composed of organic linkers and inorganic metal ions are a series of microporous crystalline materials [23,24]. Owing to the structure customizable, controlled porosity, good thermal stability and high crystallinity, MOFs were widely applied to catalysis [25,26], drug sustained release [27], gas capture [28] and sensor [29]. When MOFs were introduced into FMIPs, the response time will be cut down vastly and the fluorescence response property will be enhanced owing to their large specific surface area [30].

In this paper, an environment-friendly near infrared fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer was synthesized based on a new near infrared fluorescence CDs which were produced by succulents and zeolite imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). The NIR fluorescence carbon dots with an aggregated state were embedded into MOFs, which resulted in the sensitivity of CDs was improved by confinement effects of porous structures [31]. With the advantages of selective and rapid recognizing trypsin, the molecularly imprinted polymer based on carbon dots/zeolite imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8@MIP) was applied to detect trace trypsin in human serum and urine successfully.

Fresh succulents (breed: pachyveria pachyphytoides walth) were purchased from local flower shop. Polyvinylidene fluoride microporous filtration membrane (0.22 μm), 2-methylimidazole (HmIM), 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES, 98%), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 25%) were gain from Aladdin. Trypsin (from bovine pancreas), bovine serum albumin, bovine hemoglobin, human serum albumin, ovalbumin and lysozym were purchased from BioDuly (Nanjing, China). Anhydrous ethanol, methyl alcohol, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, nitric acid and sulfuric acid were purchased from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China). All salts were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution was prepared by mixing sodium hydrogen phosphate (0.01 M) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (0.01 M).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried on TD3500 gained from Dandong Tongda in China. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was executed on JEM 2100F instrument gained from JEOL in Japan. Fluorescence intensity of carbon dots and fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers were characterized on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer brought from Hitachi in Japan. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out on Sigma HD instrument gained from Carl Zeiss AG in Germany. Ultraviolet absorption spectrum was recorded on UV-1780 spectrophotometer obtained from Shimadzu in Japan. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas of materials were operated on NOVA 2000 gained from Quantachrome Instruments in America. Centrifugal operation was implemented on a Neofuge 15 high speed centrifuge brought from Shanghai Lishen in China, and dry operation was carried out on a DZF-6050AB vacuum oven. Energy spectrum analysis was operated by an energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS) on X-MaxN instrument brought from Oxford Instruments in England. Fourier-transform infrared spectrometry was performed on IR Affinisy-1 spectrophotometer brought from Shimadzu in Japan.

NIR CDs were synthesized according to previous report with some modifications [32]. In this work, fresh succulent was firstly used as raw material for preparing a novel NIR fluorescence carbon dot. Firstly, 5.0 g of fresh succulent pieces was mixed with 50 mL of ethanol/double distilled water solution (4/1, V/V) in a 100-mL breaker for 30 min. Then, the filter liquor was obtained with 0.22 μm microporous filtration membrane. After that, the green precursor solution was placed into a 100-mL Teflon stainless steel reactor which was put into oven at 150 °C for 6 h to obtain near infrared carbon dots (NIR CDs) solution. Finally, the NIR CDs solution was stored at 4 °C prior to further use.

ZIF-8 was synthesized by treating zinc nitrate hexahydrate with 2-methylimidazole according with previous report [33]. Briefly, 1.63 g of 2-methylimidazole and 0.73 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were added into double 50 mL of methanol, respectively. Then they were mixed and magnetically stirred at room temperature for 1 h. After that, the reaction solution was stood overnight. Then, the mixed solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed with methanol for 3 times. Finally, the obtained ZIF-8 was dried in oven at 60 °C.

20 mL of NIR CDs solution and 20 mg of ZIF-8 were firstly added into a 100-mL flask. Then, 60 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of NH3·H2O were added into the mixture solution under violently magnetic stirring for 1 h to obtain the precursor solution. Next, 10 mg of trypsin and 100 μL of APTES were mixed with the precursor solution under magnetic stirring at room temperature for 5 h. After the solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was repetitively washed with 1 M NaCl solution until no trypsin was detected in the eluent by UV–vis. Finally, the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was dried in vacuum oven at 50 °C.

The non-imprinted polymer based on CDs@ZIF-8 (CDs@ZIF-8@NIP) was prepared using the same process without adding the template molecule. The imprinted polymer based on CDs (CDs@MIP) was also synthesized by the same approach without ZIF-8.

All fluorescence intensity detection was performed on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer attached with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The slit width of was set 10 nm and the photomultiplier tube voltage was set 600 V. Firstly, 5 mg of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was dispersed into 5 mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (pH = 8, 10 mM). Then, appropriate amounts of trypsin solutions were added. After incubated for 1.5 min, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was measured at 683 nm.

To investigate the anti-interference ability of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP with various ions including Na+, Ag+, K+, NH4 +, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, NO3 −, Cl−, and SO4 2− were set as background interfering ions. Firstly, 5.0 mg of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was dispersed into 5.0 mL of PBS. Then, a certain concentration of ion solution was added. After incubated for 1.5 min, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was measured. The specificity experiment was performed by choosing bovine serum albumin, bovine hemoglobin, human serum albumin, ovalbumin and lysozyme as the competitive proteins. 0.5 ng/L of different competitive protein was added into constant concentration of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP or CDs@ZIF-8@NIP solution. After incubation for 1.5 min, the solution was measured quickly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation of human serum and urine
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy blood and urine samples were obtained from the local Hospital in Jishou. All experiments were conformed to the relevant laws and institutional guide lines of China. According to previous report [34], human serum sample was prepared by adding 15 mL acetonitrile into 10 mL of blood sample. Then it was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove large molecules and proteins in serum sample. After that, the human serum sample was diluted to 10 times with PBS (pH = 8, 10 mM) for further analysis. 10 mL of human urine was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to obtain the supernatant for further use. Then, the supernatant was diluted with PBS (the volume ratio of is 1:100) to obtain human urine sample for further experience. The real application research was studied by added different concentration of trypsin in these samples.

This work firstly prepared a novel NIR FMIP sensor for the determination of trace trypsin by combing the molecular imprinting technology with NIR CDs and ZIF-8. Firstly, this report synthesized a new NIR CDs enriched with carboxylic groups and hydroxyl groups by one step solvothermal method in Fig. 1 . Meanwhile, ZIF-8 with high specific surface area was synthesized by reacting with zinc nitrate and 2-methylimidazole at room temperature to provide an ideal platform for the formation of more imprinted sites [30]. After then, the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was prepared by sol–gel method under mild condition using APTES and TEOS as functional monomer and cross-linker, respectively. At the beginning of imprinting procedure, the NIR CDs were coated onto the surface of ZIF-8 by the cross-linking interaction of TEOS. Then, trypsin was assembled to the surface of CDs@ZIF-8 by the silanization interaction between APTES and TEOS to form the imprinted layer. Finally, the specific recognition sites matching with trypsin in shape, size and function groups were created after the template protein was removed. And the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was quenched when the template proteins were rebound to the imprinted cavities of on the surface of MIP layer [35]. While the template protein was extracted from the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP rises again. With the occurring of imprinting, fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) was changed from the red of CDs to the wathet blue of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP. It was possibly caused by the imprinted layer blocked the fluorescence emission of CDs, which also lead to the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP weak to that of CDs.

The chemical functional groups of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the adsorption peak at 3170 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C–H group. The wide absorption bands at 3420 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching and bending vibration of O–H groups [36]. The absorption bands at 1457 cm−1 and 1308 cm−1 were originated from the C-N stretching vibration of imidazole ring, and the adsorption peaks at 1175 cm−1 and 1145 cm−1 were the internal and external bending vibration of imidazole ring, and the peak of 465 cm−1 was related to Zn-N stretching vibration. The data were consistent with the reported literature [37], which indicated the ZIF-8 was successfully prepared. Compared with ZIF-8, the prepared CDs@ZIF-8@MIP had new characteristic peaks at 688 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 owing to the stretching vibration of Si-O and Si-O-Si, respectively, which proved that the MIP layer was successfully synthesized on the surface of ZIF-8 [38].

The fluorescence spectra of CDs in Fig. 2b and c showed that CDs have excitation wavelength independence property, and the optimal excitation wavelength of the CDs was concentrated at 400 nm. On the basis of the emission wavelength of CDs, the particles emitting red fluorescence at 683 nm can be used for fluorescence analysis [39]. The UV/vis absorption spectra of trypsin, CDs and CDs@trypsin (inset, ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@MIP@trypsin) were shown in Fig. 2d. The ZIF-8 displayed a weak absorption band, but the CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a strong absorption band in UV/vis range, which indicated that CDs were embedded into ZIF-8 [40]. The ultraviolet spectrum of CDs@trypsin was similar to the absorption spectrum of the CDs indicated no complex forming between CDs and trypsin [41]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2e, there is no overlap of peaks between the UV/vis absorption spectrum of trypsin and the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs, which suggested the electron transfer between the CDs and trypsin was responsible for the fluorescence quenching [42]. As seen in Fig. 2f, the electrons of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (in the valence band) accepted the UV energy, and they were excited to the conduction band. With the absence of trypsin, these electrons would return to the ground state with energy releasing, in which they were emitted fluorescence [43]. Moreover, the UV absorbance peak of trypsin is 261 nm, which is close to the excited state of CDs. With the appearance of trypsin, the amino group of APTES in the imprinted cavities of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and the carboxyl group of trypsin were bound by hydrogen bond, which could lead to the electrons of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP transition to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of trypsin [44]. As a consequence, the electron transfer contributed to the fluorescence quenching of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP with the addition of trypsin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM, TEM, XRD, EDS and BET characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were characterized by scanning electron microscope and transmission electron microscope. As shown in Fig. 3 a, ZIF-8 was prepared with the size about 20–40 nm, which was similar with the previous works [45,46]. Fig. 3b showed that the diameter of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was about 80–110 nm, which indicated the successful coating of MIP layer on the surface of ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the surface of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was rougher than that of CDs@ZIF-8@NIP, which was attributed to the existence of imprinted cavities on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP. TEM image shown in Fig. 3d indicated the size of CDs was about 11 nm. Furthermore, the TEM image of the ZIF-8 shown in Fig. 3e suggested that the ZIF-8 with a shape of rhombic dodecahedron was dispersed well. Compared with the original ZIF-8, the diameter of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP shown in Fig. 3f increased obviously, which exhibited that the imprinted layer was synthesized successfully on the surface of the ZIF-8.

The XRD patterns of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (inset, ZIF-8 and CD@ZIF-8) were shown in Fig. 3g. The XRD patterns of ZIF-8 agreed well with previous report [47], which indicated the ZIF-8 was successfully synthesized. The XRD patterns of ZIF-8 and CD@ZIF-8 exhibited that the crystal form of ZIF-8 was nearly unchanged after CDs confined into it [39]. But the XRD patterns of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were highly distinct from the ZIF-8, which suggested the degree of crystallization of ZIF-8 were changed after polymerization [48].

The EDS spectra of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were also shown in Fig. 3h and i. Both CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP contain C, N, O, Zn and Si elements. For CDs@ZIF-8@MIP, the percentages of C, N, O, Si and Zn were 48.19%, 13.21%, 25.51%, 10.91% and 2.18%, respectively. For CDs@ZIF-8@NIP, the percentages of C, N, O, Si and Zn were 29.61%, 13.28%, 38.88%, 14.12% and 4.11%. Among them, Zn element originated from ZIF-8 and Si element originated from the hydrolysis of TEOS and APTES in alkaline environment. These data also verified the presence of MIPs on the surface of ZIF-8 [49].

BET specific surface area of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@MIP, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were obtained by N2 adsorption–desorption measurement. The BET specific surface area of ZIF-8 (1501.2 m2/g) was similar to previous report [50], which indicated the successful synthesis of ZIF-8. The BET specific surface area of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (581.6 m2/g) was greater than that of CDs@MIP (90.5 m2/g) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (321.4 m2/g), which was due to the introduction of ZIF-8 with high specific area and the formation of imprinted cavities [30]. The BET specific surface area of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was smaller than that of CDs@ZIF-8 (741.7 m2/g), which was caused by the silica layer on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optosensing of CDs@ZIF-8 MIP toward trypsin
Effect of pH and incubation time
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the recognition ability of the imprinted NIR fluorescence sensor, the fluorescence intensities of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP at different pH solutions were shown in Fig. 4 a. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP decreased slowly when the pH values increased from 5.0 to 8.0, and it was stable at pH 8.0–9.0. But when the pH values were dropt down to 4.0 or rose up to 10.0, the fluorescence intensities of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP were seriously reduced, which was attributed to the solution at lower pH values dissolved the surface of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP resulting in surface defects. And the silica shell of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP can be ionized by the solution at higher pH values, which created surface defects by the nucleophilical attack from OH− [51]. The relative fluorescence intensities of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP at different pH values ranged from 5.0 to 9.0 were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4b, the maximal relative fluorescence intensity change was observed at pH 8.0, which was owing to that solution at pH less than 8.0 or pH > 8.0 decreased the interaction between binding sites on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and trypsin. In addition, the solution at lower and higher pH value may affect the activity of trypsin [42]. Therefore, pH 8.0 was selected for further experiments.

The fluorescence intensity changes of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP at different incubation times were displayed in Fig. 4c. The response time of both CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward trypsin was 1.5 min. However, the fluorescence change value of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was higher than that of CDs@ZIF-8@NIP, which was ascribed to there are two effects involving specific and non-specific recognition on the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin. The changes on fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@MIP were shown in Fig. 4d. The change of fluorescence intensity of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin was higher than that of the CDs@MIP, which indicated the ZIF-8, one of the most popular MOFs materials, can significantly improve the binding capacity and mass transfer rate between CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and trypsin [30].

The recognition abilities of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward trypsin with the different concentrations range of 0–9.0 ng/L were investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 5 a and b. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was quenched gradually with the increment of trypsin concentrations, which was owing to these imprinted cavities complemented with trypsin in size, structure and function groups on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP. When trypsin molecules were bound to these imprinted sites, the electrons of the NIR CDs were transferred to trypsin, which lead to the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP [52]. The fluorescence quenching effect of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin with different concentrations followed the Stern-Volmer equation [53].
F0/F = 1 + KSV·[C]
where F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP, and F is the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP in trypsin solution. KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [C] is the concentration of trypsin. As shown in Fig. 5c, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed excellent linear fluorescence quenching toward trypsin in the concentration range of 0.05 – 9.0 ng/L with the linear equation of F0/F = 0.0725[C] + 0.0127. While for CDs@ZIF-8@NIP, the linear equation was F0/F = 0.0255[C] + 0.00602. The imprinting factor (IF) which is defined as the ratio of the KSV,MIP and KSV,NIP (KSV,MIP and KSV,NIP are the linear slopes of equation) was used to evaluate the specific imprinting effect of the CD@ZIF-8@MIP. According with the slopes of curve A and B in Fig. 5c, the IF was calculated as 2.84, which indicated that CDs@ZIF-8@MIP had specific adsorption toward trypsin. The limit of detection (LOD) of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin was calculated as 8.8 pg/L following 3σ/S. Here, σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8 MIP for trypsin
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of the imprinted sensor toward trypsin, the fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward 0.5 ng/L trypsin in the presence of different interfering substances were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, compared with the fluorescence change for trypsin, no apparent decrease of fluorescence intensity was observed for Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Na+, Cu2+, K+, Ag+, NH4 +, Cl−, NO3 − and SO4 2−. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin was higher than that toward these interfering ions. These data suggested that theses tested ions can’t affect the recognition of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward trypsin.

In order to further evaluate the selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP, the fluorescence quenching effects of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward 0.5 ng/L of trypsin, bovine serum albumin, bovine hemoglobin, lysozyme, ovalbumin and human serum albumin were shown in Fig. 6c. The CDs@ZIF-8@MIP gave the highest fluorescence quenching effect toward trypsin compared with other structural analogs, but the CDs@ZIF-8@NIP showed a similar response to all of these analytes. The data indicated that the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed high selectivity toward trypsin, which was attributed to there are imprinted cavities matching with trypsin on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP. The molecular weight of lysozyme (MW = 14,000) was far less than trypsin (MW = 24,000), which made lysozyme easy to get into and out cavities of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP. However, the molecular weight of bovine serum albumin (MW = 66,340), bovine hemoglobin (MW = 64,500), ovalbumin (MW = 44,500) and human serum albumin (MW = 67,000) were too large to approach these imprinted holes.

The accuracy of this sensing strategy was performed in human serum and urine. Different concentrations of trypsin were added in serum and urine samples and the concentration of trypsin was calculated according to the Stern-Volmer plots. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of trypsin in human serum and urine ranged from 95.3 to 104.1% and 97.7–105.2% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 2.1–4.3% and 2.5–3.3%, respectively, which indicated the practicable application of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor for trypsin assay in clinical diagnosis.

The performance comparisons of different methods for the detection of trypsin were shown in Table 2 . The prepared NIR CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor possessed with a very low detection limit, which is better than those of the other reported methods. Meanwhile, the NIR CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor with high sensitivity was prepared under a simple, mild and green synthetic procedure.

The reproducibility experiment was performed using seven batches of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP prepared by same procedure and each batch was measured in replicate for five cycles of template adsorption–desorption. The fluorescence intensities of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP remained 90.3% of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP’s original fluorescence intensities with an RSD less than 2.3%, which suggested that the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP behaved better reproducibility [16].

The stability experiment was carried out by measuring the fluorescence intensities of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP with the absence and presence of trypsin in every 10 min repeatedly under the optimized conditions. Experiment results exhibited that the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP unchanged obviously in 60 min, which indicated the successful preparation of imprinted layer covered on the surface of CDs to keep the stable fluorescence emission of CDs [54].

An eco-friendly NIR CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor was prepared by using NIR carbon dots as the fluorescent source and ZIF-8 as the carriers for sensitive and selective recognition of trace trypsin in this paper. With outstanding anti-interference ability, rapid response speed, and low detection limit, the NIR CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor was successfully applied for the determination of trace trypsin in real samples. The eco-friendly NIR CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor provides a green, low-cost and sensitive method for determining trace proteins rapidly.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 21565014), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan province (No.2020JJ4500) and the Innovation Fund Designated for Graduate Students of Jishou University (JDY19014).

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

Biosens. Bioelectron.
